perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definition of "Confidential Information" to include only information explicitly identified as confidential can contradict trade secret laws that protect information even if not explicitly marked as confidential, depending on the context and jurisdiction (e.g., if reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy).
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition contradicts the UTSA (as adopted in many US states), which protects trade secrets even if not explicitly labelled as such, provided reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy. The original definition includes 'any such information regardless of whether such information is identified as confidential or not'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly alters the definition of "Confidential Information" by requiring explicit identification as confidential, directly contradicting trade secret laws. According to the provided Perturbation Explanation, trade secret laws protect information even if not explicitly marked as confidential, provided reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy. The original text explicitly includes information 'regardless of whether such information is identified as confidential or not.' The changed text removes this protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'trade secret,' states that it includes various forms of information if '(A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value...from not being generally known.' This snippet implies that explicit labeling is not a prerequisite for trade secret protection, as long as reasonable measures for secrecy are taken. By requiring explicit identification, the modified contract weakens the protection of trade secrets and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text:
5.1. Recipient shall:
5.1.1. hold Confidential Information in confidence and protect it by using at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Recipient uses to safeguard its own confidential or proprietary information of a like nature from unauthorized use, disclosure or dissemination;
perturbation.changed_text:
5.1. Recipient shall:
5.1.1. hold Confidential Information in confidence and protect it by using a minimal degree of care, regardless of how Recipient safeguards its own confidential or proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the recipient should use a 'minimal' degree of care rather than a 'reasonable degree of care,' there will be a contradiction of the implied duty to protect confidential information with a level of diligence appropriate to its nature, which is often implied in non-disclosure agreements and protected by legal precedent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification requiring only 'minimal' care contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. A minimal level of care might not be considered reasonable or in good faith, especially when dealing with sensitive confidential information, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: duty of good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you duty of good faith The duty of good faith is the principle that directors and officers of a corporation who are making decisions in their capacities as corporate fiduciaries , must act with a conscious regard for their responsibilities in that role. A violation of the duty of good faith may include an intentional neglect of the usual duties of a director or officer, intentionally acting for a purpose other than the benefit of the corporation, or intentionally violating the law. Though there is no private shareholder right of action for a violation of the duty of good faith, its violation may also raise a claim under the duty of loyalty . In Heritage Surveyors & Eng'rs, Inc. v. Nat'l Penn Bank, 801 A.2d 1248 , the court held that “the duty of good faith has been defined as honesty in fact in the conduct or transaction concerned”. Furthermore, the Uniform Commercial Code section 1-304 provides that “Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.” [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law corporations wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The modified text mandates a 'minimal' degree of care, conflicting with the original text's requirement for 'at least the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care.' This directly undermines the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably. Snippet 1 defines the duty of good faith as 'honesty in fact in the conduct or transaction concerned' and states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Requiring only 'minimal' care likely violates this duty, as it is not reasonable or in good faith, creating a contradiction because 'minimal' care is a deviation from acting reasonably and honestly in protecting sensitive information.

perturbation.original_text:
7.3. Continuing Obligations
7.3.1. The obligations of confidentiality, non-disclosure and non-use set forth in this Confidentiality Agreement shall survive the termination or expiration of this Confidentiality Agreement (i) with respect to any Confidential Information that constitutes a trade secret under applicable law, for so long as such item shall continue to constitute a trade secret under applicable law, and (ii) with respect to any Confidential Information that does not constitute a trade secret under applicable law, for a period of 5 (five) years from and after the date of disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text:
7.3. Continuing Obligations
7.3.1. The obligations of confidentiality, non-disclosure and non-use set forth in this Confidentiality Agreement shall survive the termination or expiration of this Confidentiality Agreement (i) with respect to any Confidential Information that constitutes a trade secret under applicable law, for a period of 1 year, and (ii) with respect to any Confidential Information that does not constitute a trade secret under applicable law, for a period of 5 (five) years from and after the date of disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Trade secrets are meant to be protected as long as it remains a trade secret. By limiting the protection to a year for a trade secret is contradictory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification limits the confidentiality of trade secrets to one year after termination, contradicting the standard legal protection for trade secrets, which extends for as long as the information remains a trade secret and is properly protected.  A trade secret can be protected indefinitely as long as it is kept secret and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the trade secret protection to one year, directly contradicting the established legal principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' in 18 U.S. Code § 1839 as information that derives economic value from not being generally known and for which the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep secret. This definition implies indefinite protection as long as secrecy is maintained, which is violated by limiting the protection to one year. The change undermines the very essence of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text:
10. GOVERNING LAW AND DISPUTE RESOLUTION
10.1. Governing Law
10.1.1. The laws of Denmark govern the Agreement and any legal dispute arising out of or in relation to the Agreement must be solved in accordance with the laws of Denmark.
perturbation.changed_text:
10. GOVERNING LAW AND DISPUTE RESOLUTION
10.1. Governing Law
10.1.1. The laws of Denmark govern the Agreement and any legal dispute arising out of or in relation to the Agreement must be solved in accordance with the laws of the United States.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Denmark to the United States, while the agreement is clearly drafted with a Danish entity ('Nordic Knowledge Partners ApS Central Business Register (CVR) number: 36428198 Dampfærgevej 9, 1. 2100 -DK Copenhagen') introduces a direct contradiction. International contract law dictates that choice of law clauses should be respected unless there are compelling reasons to disregard them. Such a sudden change introduces uncertainty and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change to US law contradicts the principle in PICC Article 3.1, which supports party autonomy in choosing the governing law. While not legally binding in the same way as a statute, PICC reflects widely accepted principles of international contract law, and unilaterally changing the governing law creates a conflict and potential grounds for challenging the enforceability of the clause.

perturbation.justification: The modification changing the governing law from Denmark to the United States introduces a contradiction based on the principle of party autonomy in choosing the governing law, as supported by the Law Explanation referencing PICC Article 3.1. While the provided scraped snippets do not explicitly mention this principle, the Law Explanation connects the change to a violation of widely accepted principles of international contract law, suggesting the modified clause undermines the original intent and potentially leads to unenforceability.
